label: Sample Complexity
definition: The number of training examples a learning algorithm needs to reach a target accuracy with a target confidence. It quantifies the data cost of learning and is governed by the capacity of the model class.

body.text: Sample complexity is the data-cost question of machine learning: how many labeled examples does an algorithm need before it can reliably learn a concept? Formally it is the number of training samples required to reach a chosen accuracy with a chosen confidence, and it makes precise the intuition that some problems are easy to learn from a handful of cases while others demand vast datasets. Where computational complexity counts operations, sample complexity counts examples, and in the data-hungry regime of modern machine learning it is often the binding constraint.

The notion gets its rigorous meaning inside the PAC Learning framework, whose parameters it directly inherits. To be Probably Approximately Correct, a learner must achieve error below a tolerance epsilon with probability at least one minus delta, and the sample complexity is the count of examples sufficient to meet both targets. Standard results show this count growing polynomially in one over epsilon and logarithmically in one over delta, so demanding higher accuracy is expensive while demanding higher confidence is cheap. This asymmetry, that tightening the error bar costs far more data than tightening the failure probability, is a recurring feature of learning-theoretic bounds.

The dominant factor in sample complexity is the capacity of the hypothesis class, measured by the VC Dimension. The fundamental theorem of statistical learning shows that the number of samples needed scales roughly linearly with the VC dimension: a more expressive model class, able to fit more patterns, requires proportionally more data to pin down the right hypothesis and to keep the Generalization gap small. This is the formal version of the everyday observation that complex models need more data. It is also why Empirical Risk Minimization is trustworthy only once the sample size exceeds the level set by the class capacity; below it, low training error says little about true error.

Sample complexity explains why Inductive Bias is so valuable in practice. A well-chosen bias shrinks the effective hypothesis class, lowers its capacity, and so reduces the number of examples needed to learn, which is precisely why convolutional structure makes vision models learn from fewer images and why strong priors enable few-shot learning. The No Free Lunch Theorem sets the boundary on this gain: no bias lowers sample complexity across all problems at once, only on those the bias actually fits, so data efficiency is bought with assumptions and paid for elsewhere. Pursuing better sample efficiency, through transfer learning, pretraining, active learning, and richer priors, is one of the central engineering goals of the field, since data is frequently the scarcest and most expensive resource of all.

image.alt: Two learning-curve plots of accuracy against the number of training samples, one for a Gaussian naive Bayes classifier and one for an SVM. In each, a blue training-score curve and an orange cross-validation-score curve are drawn with shaded standard-deviation bands. The training and cross-validation curves start far apart and converge as the number of training examples grows.
image.caption: A learning curve shows how accuracy depends on the amount of data. With few examples the training score is high but the cross-validation score is low, a large generalization gap; as more examples are added the two curves converge. Sample complexity is the number of examples needed to close that gap and reach a target accuracy, and the curve makes that data cost visible for each model.
